mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Belgium on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe. Belgium is a small country in the west of Europe, on the North Sea, between France and the Netherlands 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Belgium?
mapQuestions.answer: Brussels. It is also the de facto capital of the European Union, and home to NATO too.

mapQuestions.question: How many official languages does Belgium have?
mapQuestions.answer: Three. Dutch (called Flemish here), French, and German. The country is divided up by language.

mapQuestions.question: How big is Belgium?
mapQuestions.answer: Around 30,000 square kilometres, about the same as Wales or Maryland. About 11.7 million people live in it, making it one of Europe's most densely populated countries.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest point in Belgium?
mapQuestions.answer: The Signal de Botrange in the Ardennes forest in the east, only 694 metres. Belgium is one of the flattest countries in Europe.

foods.name: Belgian frites
foods.description: Crispy thick-cut chips, fried twice in beef fat, served in a paper cone with a small fork and a dollop of mayonnaise. Belgians invented these — not the French.

foods.name: Belgian chocolate
foods.description: Belgium produces 220,000 tonnes of chocolate a year. Belgians invented the praline (a chocolate with a filling). Only cocoa butter is used — no vegetable fat. The standard is fierce.

foods.name: Belgian waffle
foods.description: Two main kinds: Brussels (rectangular, lighter, dusted with powdered sugar) and Liège (oval, denser, with caramelised sugar pockets baked into it). Eaten with strawberries, chocolate or whipped cream.

foods.name: Moules-frites
foods.description: A whole pot of steamed mussels (often cooked in white wine and onions or with celery) served with a big cone of frites. The unofficial national dish.

foods.name: Carbonade flamande
foods.description: A Flemish beef stew slow-cooked in Belgian beer with onions and a slice of mustardy bread to thicken the sauce. Comfort food in a bowl.

foods.name: Speculoos
foods.description: A thin, crunchy spiced biscuit (cinnamon, nutmeg, cloves), often pressed with windmill or saint shapes. Sold as Lotus Biscoff abroad, but in Belgium they appear with every coffee.

scavengerItems.label: A "Frituur" / "Friterie" shop selling frites in paper cones
A Belgian chocolatier window full of pralines
A waffle iron or street waffle cart
A Tintin, Smurfs or Lucky Luke comic in a window or shop
The Manneken Pis statue (the little peeing boy fountain in Brussels)
The Atomium, the giant silver atom-shaped building in Brussels
A medieval belfry tower (Bruges, Ghent — UNESCO listed)
Cobblestone streets crossing canals (Bruges or Ghent)
A bike with a basket leaning against an old building
A bilingual sign in BOTH Dutch and French (Brussels)
A diamond shop in the Antwerp diamond district
A Trappist monastery sign or a tall monastery glass of beer

geoMatches.description: The capital. Bilingual (Dutch + French). Home of the EU institutions, NATO, Tintin and giant chocolate shops. Wide, slightly chaotic, surprisingly multicultural.
A medieval town in Flanders, with stone canals, swans, a 13th-century belfry tower and chocolate shops down every street. Looks frozen in 1450.
Another beautiful medieval Flemish city, less touristy than Bruges. Big student population, gothic spires, and the largest collection of medieval graffiti carved into stone walls in Europe.
A port and fashion city in Flanders. The world's diamond capital — around 85% of the world's rough diamonds pass through Antwerp's diamond district every year.
The French-speaking south. Rolling forests in the Ardennes, deep river valleys, World War 1 and 2 battlefields, and quiet farms with small breweries.
A small town in the Ardennes that gave its name to every "spa" in the world. People have been bathing in its hot springs for over 400 years.

senses.smell: Belgian chocolate on every street, frites bubbling in hot beef fat, sweet waffle batter, malty beer cellars, North Sea mussels boiling, summer rain on cobblestones…
senses.hear: Dutch and French in the same conversation, bicycle bells, the chimes of carillons (bell towers playing music), tram bells, "Hallo!", "Bonjour!", the clink of beer glasses…
senses.taste: Crispy hot frites with mayo, melting praline chocolate, warm Liège waffle with caramelised sugar pockets, spiced crunchy speculoos, sweet mussel broth…
senses.feel: Cold drizzle (Belgium's default weather), the warmth of a Brussels chocolate shop, smooth chocolate melting on your fingers, cobblestones rolling under your shoes, soft Belgian lace…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How is Belgium the chocolate capital of the world?
stories.body: Belgium produces around 220,000 tonnes of chocolate every year. The country has over 2,000 chocolate shops, more per person than anywhere else on Earth. A Belgian called Jean Neuhaus II invented the "praline" — a chocolate with a soft filling inside — in Brussels in 1912. Belgian chocolatiers are obsessive: they use only cocoa butter (no cheap vegetable fats), no artificial flavours, and grind cocoa beans for hours to make their chocolate silky-smooth. Brussels Airport is the world's biggest single chocolate-selling location, with around 800 tonnes sold to travellers every year. If you eat a real Belgian praline and then a cheap chocolate, the difference is shocking.

stories.question: Why are so many famous comic-book characters Belgian?
stories.body: Belgium is the home of European comics, called "bandes dessinées" or BD. Tintin, the boy reporter with the cowlick and his dog Snowy, was created by Belgian artist Hergé in 1929 and has been translated into over 70 languages. The Smurfs (tiny blue people in a forest mushroom village) — Belgian, created by Peyo in 1958. Lucky Luke (the cowboy who shoots faster than his shadow) — Belgian. Brussels has the Comics Art Museum, and many walls across the city are painted with huge murals of these characters. Belgian comics are taught in schools as serious literature, with their own scholarly journals.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Belgium is tiny but invented some of the most famous comic characters. What other things do small countries make a huge cultural mark with?

stories.question: Why is there a famous statue of a peeing boy?
stories.body: Brussels' most famous statue is Manneken Pis, a small bronze figure of a little boy peeing into a fountain. He is only 61 cm tall and has stood there since at least 1388. There are several legends about him. The most popular: during a siege of the city in the 1100s, a small Brussels boy peed on a lit fuse and saved the town from being blown up. The Manneken now owns over 1,000 costumes (kept in the City Museum, like a tiny royal wardrobe). He gets dressed in different outfits for different occasions — Santa for Christmas, Mickey Mouse for Disneyland anniversaries, even a coronavirus-mask outfit in 2020. He also has a sister statue (Jeanneke Pis) and a dog statue (Het Zinneke).

stories.question: Why does Belgium have three languages?
stories.body: Belgium has been a battleground between French and Germanic Europe for over a thousand years. Today the country is officially divided into three language regions. Flanders, in the north, speaks Dutch (called "Flemish"). Wallonia, in the south, speaks French. A small region in the east speaks German. Brussels, in the middle, is officially bilingual — every street sign, government document, restaurant menu, and metro announcement must be in both Dutch and French. The language tension is so strong that Belgium has at times almost split into two separate countries. It also has six different governments (federal, regional and community), which makes it one of the most complex political setups in the world. Yet most days, Belgians just get on with their chocolate, frites and beer.

stories.question: Why does Brussels matter so much for Europe?
stories.body: Brussels is the de facto capital of the European Union, a club of 27 countries representing around 450 million people. About 25,000 EU staff work in the city, plus another 30,000+ diplomats, lobbyists and journalists. The EU parliament has buildings in Brussels — and confusingly in Strasbourg, France, which French politicians refuse to give up, so MEPs shuttle between the two cities once a month at a cost of around €200 million a year. NATO, the military alliance of 32 Western countries, is also based in Brussels. All of this from a country smaller than Wales. Brussels is the city where rules are made that affect what is in your phone, what is on your food label, and what plastics can be sold in your country.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Should a country smaller than Wales really be the centre of decisions for 450 million people? Or is small actually clever?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best chocolate you have eaten in Belgium?
Frites + mayo: weird, wonderful, or wrong?
Bruges, Ghent, Brussels, Antwerp — which Belgian city has been your favourite?
Have you spotted signs in two languages today? Did anyone confuse you by switching mid-sentence?
Belgium invented Tintin AND the Smurfs. Which character has surprised you the most here?
Belgium is tiny but punches huge — chocolate, beer, comics, EU. What does that tell you about the size of a country?

wordSearch.hint: Belgian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Feral pigeon
animals.description: Grand-Place pigeons in Brussels expect their share of your frites.

animals.name: Mallard duck
animals.description: Paddling on Bruges canals and Ghent's rivers.

animals.name: Belgian sheepdog
animals.description: Tervuren or Groenendael — long-haired black or fawn herding dogs.

animals.name: Mute swan
animals.description: Bruges canals are famous for their swans, kept since the 1400s.

animals.name: Eurasian magpie
animals.description: Chatty black-and-white bird in every Belgian back garden.

animals.name: Red deer
animals.description: In the Ardennes forests in the south of the country.
